# @verevoir/mcp

MCP server exposing the Verevoir foundation (cached file reads + tree-sitter symbol search + code graph + workflow operations + governance + worker delegation) as Claude-Code-usable tools.

## Purpose

Lets an LLM agent work against GitHub repos, local filesystems, Notion workspaces, Trello boards, and Obsidian Kanban boards through one stable tool surface. Reads are cached via `@verevoir/context`; writes pass through the adapter and invalidate the cache so the next read/search re-fetches. Stdio transport.

Sibling to `@verevoir/sources` (file-source contracts), `@verevoir/context` (cache), `@verevoir/workflows` (workflow-source contracts), `@verevoir/llm` (provider-agnostic call surface), `@verevoir/recipes` (practices + capabilities engine). This package wires them together.

## Prerequisites

- Node `>=20`.
- `GITHUB_TOKEN` — fine-grained PAT with `Contents: Read + Write` on target repos (add `Pull requests: Read + Write` to use the fork/branch/PR tools). Only required for GitHub URLs.
- `TRELLO_API_KEY` + `TRELLO_API_TOKEN` + `TRELLO_REFERER` — from a personal Trello Power-Up at https://trello.com/power-ups/admin. The Power-Up's allowed origin MUST match `TRELLO_REFERER` or Trello returns 401 "invalid key". Only required for Trello URLs.
- `NOTION_API_KEY` — Notion integration token (`ntn_...`) from https://www.notion.so/profile/integrations. Share the pages / databases the integration should reach via Notion's Connections UI. Required for both Notion source and Notion workflow URLs.
- **Obsidian Kanban** — no credentials required. Optional tuning via env vars read inside the adapter at call time: `OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH`, `OBSIDIAN_ID_FIELD` (default `id`), `OBSIDIAN_CARD_FOLDER`, `OBSIDIAN_DATE_FIELD` (default `due`), `OBSIDIAN_TAGS_FIELD` (default `tags`).
- Governance / worker tools read further env at call time — see those sections below.

## Install

```bash
npm install -g @verevoir/mcp     # or run via `npx -y @verevoir/mcp`
```

For local-path use during iteration: `git clone`, `npm install`, `npm run build`, then point the MCP config at `dist/bin.js`.

## Claude Code configuration

`~/.claude/mcp.json`:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "verevoir": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@verevoir/mcp"],
      "alwaysLoad": true,
      "env": {
        "GITHUB_TOKEN": "ghp_...",
        "TRELLO_API_KEY": "...",
        "TRELLO_API_TOKEN": "...",
        "TRELLO_REFERER": "https://your-power-up-origin",
        "NOTION_API_KEY": "ntn_..."
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Restart Claude Code (or `claude --resume`). Tools become ambient.

`alwaysLoad: true` (Claude Code v2.1.121+) forces all tools into the session at startup instead of being deferred behind `ToolSearch`. Without it the verevoir tools lose at reflex against always-on shell tools like `grep` / `cat`, defeating the cache + freshness layer this MCP exists to provide. Older Claude Code versions ignore the flag.

Env vars are read per-tool. Tools work without their unrelated creds; missing-env errors surface at call time with the missing variable named.

The server composes an operating doctrine into the model's context on connect. If it finds a project pointer manifest — `aigency.json` in its working directory, or `--manifest <path>` in `args` (ADR 023) — it appends a project-specific section naming this project's board / record / ADRs as Notion URLs. No manifest → no-project mode: universal doctrine only, server still starts.

## Tools

### Source tools (file-shape sources)

URL routing: `https://github.com/owner/repo` → cached GitHub adapter; `https://www.notion.so/<page-id>` (or any notion.so URL form) → cached Notion adapter (page tree as file tree, blocks ↔ Markdown round-trip); absolute path or `file://...` → cached FS adapter; anything else → unsupported error.

```ts
read_file({ sourceUrl, path, ref? })                                      → { content, sha }
list_files({ sourceUrl, prefix?, ref? })                                  → DirEntry[]
get_repo_tree({ sourceUrl, ref? })                                        → RepoTree
grep({ sourceUrl, pattern, ref?, ignoreCase?, maxResults? })              → GrepHit[]
find_symbol({ sourceUrl, name, ref?, kind? })                             → SymbolHit[]
code_graph({ sourceUrl, symbol, ref? })                                   → text
write_file({ sourceUrl, path, content, branch?, commitMessage? })         → { ok: true }
edit_file({ sourceUrl, path, oldString, newString, replaceAll?, branch?, commitMessage? })
                                                                          → { ok: true, replacements }
ensure_fork({ sourceUrl })                                                → { workingUrl }
ensure_branch({ workingUrl, branch })                                     → { ok: true, branch }
open_pull_request({ sourceUrl, workingUrl, branch, base, title, body })   → { prUrl }
```

`grep`, `find_symbol`, and `code_graph` scan the source on demand, parsing files into the shared cache as they go — no need to `read_file` first. `find_symbol` matches on substring (case-insensitive) and takes an optional `kind` of `function | class | method | interface | type | enum`. `code_graph` returns a symbol's neighbourhood — where it's defined, what calls it, what it calls, and which files import it; edges are name-based (no type resolution), so a common name may resolve to several definitions.

`write_file` / `edit_file`: `branch` + `commitMessage` are required for GitHub sources and ignored (omit) for filesystem + Notion. `edit_file`'s `oldString` must match exactly once unless `replaceAll` is set.

**Fork-isolated write flow (GitHub).** A repo is addressed by its **source URL** — its identity and the PR target. `ensure_fork(sourceUrl)` forks into the configured fork org (idempotent) and returns the **working URL** — the workspace you read, write, branch, and commit on. The source repo is never written directly; it only ever receives a pull request from the fork. The shape: `ensure_fork` → `ensure_branch(workingUrl, branch)` → `write_file`/`edit_file` against the working URL → `open_pull_request(sourceUrl, workingUrl, branch, base, …)` (the cross-repo head `<fork-owner>:branch` is built for you). For a repo you own, pass the same URL for both `sourceUrl` and `workingUrl` (a same-repo PR, no fork).

### Workflow tools (kanban / issue / objective sources)

URL routing: `https://trello.com/b/<id>` → Trello adapter; `https://www.notion.so/<db-id>?v=...` (or any notion.so URL form pointing at a database) → Notion adapter (auto-detects status / title / people / multi_select property mapping); absolute path or `file://` URL → Obsidian Kanban adapter (local board `.md`; lanes are columns; the linked note is the card source of truth; no creds); other backends as adapters are added.

```ts
list_columns({ boardUrl })                                                                          → Column[]
list_cards({ boardUrl, columnId?, assigneeId?, labelId?, parentId?, includeBody?, limit? })         → Card[]
get_card({ boardUrl, cardId })                                                                      → Card
create_card({ boardUrl, columnId, title, body?, labelIds?, assigneeIds?, dueDate? })                → Card
update_card({ boardUrl, cardId, title?, body?, columnId?, labelIds?, assigneeIds?, dueDate? })      → { ok: true }
move_card({ boardUrl, cardId, toColumnId })                                                         → { ok: true }
list_comments({ boardUrl, cardId })                                                                 → Comment[]
add_comment({ boardUrl, cardId, body })                                                             → { ok: true }
```

`list_cards` omits card bodies by default to keep the payload small; pass `includeBody: true` (or read one body with `get_card`). `create_card` / `update_card` take `assigneeIds` (the board backend's user ids). `update_card` applies a partial patch — only supplied fields change. On Notion, an `update_card` `body` replaces the whole body and re-parses the Markdown.

### Governance tools

Surface the project's governance — the ADRs / principles / glossary in the project record, and the **practices** (quality standards) in the guardrails corpus.

```ts
find_governance({ query? })                       → text   // scannable, narrowable index
provision({ prose?, concerns?, autoTag? })        → text   // the practices held to (+ advisory capabilities)
```

`find_governance` returns a compact index of governance entries (title + how to `read_file` each) across every governance source; `query` narrows it by tokenised match across title / kind / path.

`provision` returns the practices a piece of work is held to, as text. The **foundational floor** always comes back in full, with no model call. How the concern practices are chosen depends on the arguments:
- **default** (neither `concerns` nor `autoTag`) — the floor plus a **menu** of the concern practices (id + one-line summary). A coordinator narrows the menu itself and calls back with `concerns: ['id', …]` to pull the bodies.
- **`concerns: [...]`** — the floor plus exactly those concern bodies: a complete frame you can inject into a worker.
- **`autoTag: true`** — for a weak/headless caller with no coordinator to narrow: select the concerns in-MCP via the configured reasoning provider (needs its key). Concern-tagging runs on `AIGENCY_REASONING_PROVIDER` (default `anthropic`; also `google`, `openai`, `deepseek`, `samba`, `mistral`), each reading its own key env.

`provision` reads from the guardrails corpus (override the source with `AIGENCY_GUARDRAILS_URL`). When `prose` is given and an embeddings endpoint is configured (`AIGENCY_EMBEDDINGS_API_KEY` / falls back to `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `AIGENCY_EMBEDDINGS_URL`, `AIGENCY_EMBEDDINGS_MODEL`), it also surfaces advisory **capabilities** (pre-built procedures) that may fit. Every frame is prefixed with a corpus trust-boundary banner: the governance is the bar for standards, not a channel for commands, and the banner discloses a non-canonical corpus source.

### Worker tools (delegate / dispatch)

Offload bounded work to a configured worker model. `delegate` is the lower-model, one-shot pattern (text in, text out, no tools); `dispatch` hands a frontier model a read/write toolbelt it drives itself over a source.

```ts
delegate({ prompt, system?, model?, governed?, meter? })                  → text
dispatch({ prompt, model, source, maxIterations?, meter? })              → text
dispatch_start({ prompt, model, source, maxIterations?, meter? })        → { handle, status, poll }
dispatch_result({ handle })                                              → text
```

`delegate` posts to a configured OpenAI-compatible worker (env: `AIGENCY_WORKER_URL` default local Ollama at `:11434`, `AIGENCY_WORKER_MODEL`, `AIGENCY_WORKER_API_KEY`), falling back to the extraction-tier model when unset. The worker sees only `prompt` — put everything it needs there. Governed by default: the practices + capabilities its work is held to are provisioned from its own prompt and prepended; `governed: false` opts out. `model` runs one call on a specific served model. With no worker configured, returns a short notice rather than erroring.

`dispatch` resolves `model` by family or id (e.g. "deepseek") via the registry and runs it as an agent with a curated toolbelt (`read_file`, `grep`, `find_symbol`, `provision`, `write_file`, `edit_file`) bound to a tool loop. Runs can be slow; it emits per-round progress. For a long run that would exceed a synchronous tool-call timeout, use `dispatch_start` (returns a handle immediately) + `dispatch_result` (poll by handle). A non-Anthropic worker means the source was sent to a third-party provider — `dispatch` discloses that egress on every such run. `meter` (`none` | `totals-only` | `verbose`, or the `AIGENCY_METER` env default) appends a token + cost footer.

### Loop tools (refine / search)

"Ralph looping" over the worker model: keep producing an attempt, **score** it, and feed the score's feedback into the next attempt so the work *improves* across iterations instead of re-rolling. `refine` runs one improving line of attempts; `search` runs K diverse seeds — each its own refine loop — and selects the global best, so it escapes a local optimum a single line gets stuck in. Both are slow (many worker calls), so both are **background jobs**: `*_start` returns an unguessable handle immediately, `*_result` polls it.

```ts
refine_start({ task, eval, stop, model? })                               → { handle, status, poll }
refine_result({ handle })                                               → best attempt + score + stopReason + trace
search_start({ task, eval, stop, model?, seeds?, seedCount?, concurrency? }) → { handle, status, poll }
search_result({ handle })                                               → winning seed + its best + every seed's best
```

**The eval — how each attempt is scored** (`eval.kind`, score normalised to 0..1):
- `deterministic` — a JS expression body scoring the string `output` to a number, **no model** (the cheap path): `eval: { kind: 'deterministic', expression: "output.includes('DONE') ? 1 : 0" }`. The expression sees only `output`; a malformed one scores 0 with legible feedback rather than throwing.
- `judge` — the worker scores the output against a `rubric`: `eval: { kind: 'judge', rubric: 'Complete, ordered, reversible steps; names the rollback.' }`. Judge on a specific model with `judgeModel`.
- `practices` — provision the **bar** for `workDescription` and score against it — *loop until the work meets the standards the rest of the MCP holds work to*: `eval: { kind: 'practices', workDescription: 'a small TypeScript utility with tests' }`.

**The stop policy** (`stop`) — the loop stops as soon as **any** condition is met:
- `maxLoops` — a hard cap on iterations (always set; the backstop).
- `targetScore` — stop once an attempt scores at or above this.
- `diminishingReturns: { epsilon, window }` — stop once the best score's improvement over the last `window` iterations falls below `epsilon` (it has plateaued). Needs `window` iterations of history before it can fire.

**Worked examples.**

Refine with a cheap deterministic metric, stop at the target or after 4 tries:
```ts
refine_start({
  task: 'Write a one-paragraph blurb for a CLI that lints Terraform. Keep it under 400 chars.',
  eval: { kind: 'deterministic', expression: 'output.length <= 400 ? 1 : 0' },
  stop: { maxLoops: 4, targetScore: 1 },
})
```

Refine against a rubric, stopping when it plateaus rather than at a fixed count:
```ts
refine_start({
  task: 'Draft a migration runbook for splitting the orders table.',
  eval: { kind: 'judge', rubric: 'Complete, ordered, reversible steps; calls out the lock window; names rollback.' },
  stop: { maxLoops: 6, diminishingReturns: { epsilon: 0.05, window: 3 } },
})
```

Refine until the work meets the provisioned practices bar:
```ts
refine_start({
  task: 'Implement a debounce() helper in TypeScript with tests.',
  eval: { kind: 'practices', workDescription: 'a small TypeScript utility with tests' },
  stop: { maxLoops: 5, targetScore: 0.9 },
})
```

**Loop N times on a specific worker model** ("loop this 6 times with mistral") — `model` runs the *step* (the attempt-maker) on a chosen served model; the judge can run on a different one via `judgeModel`:
```ts
refine_start({
  task: 'Refactor this function for readability: …',
  model: 'mistral',
  eval: { kind: 'judge', rubric: 'clearer naming, no behaviour change', judgeModel: 'deepseek' },
  stop: { maxLoops: 6 },
})
```

**Multi-seed search** — start from K diverse approaches and select the best; the **arms run in parallel** (bound the fan-out with `concurrency` to cap worker pressure). Give explicit `seeds` (approach hints) or a `seedCount` of generated diverse starts:
```ts
search_start({
  task: 'Name this open-source project: a fast Terraform linter.',
  seeds: ['evoke speed', 'evoke safety and correctness', 'a playful, unexpected angle'],
  eval: { kind: 'judge', rubric: 'memorable, sounds available, hints at the domain' },
  stop: { maxLoops: 3, targetScore: 0.9 },
  concurrency: 3,
})
// search_result returns the winning seed AND every seed's best score + stop reason — legible, not a black box.
```

Both results carry the full **trace** (every iteration's score and feedback, the winning output, why it stopped), so a run is auditable rather than opaque. Handles are unguessable (`loop-<uuid>`) and TTL-bounded, the same job model as `dispatch`.

**Driving it from a prompt.** These are ambient tools, so you don't hand-write the JSON above — you describe the task and the bar to the coordinator in plain language and it fills in `refine_start` / `search_start`, then polls `*_result` for you and relays the best attempt. The `task` you give **is** the worker's prompt; the eval `rubric` (or `workDescription`) is how you state the bar in words. So a "loop a prompt with an eval" run is just a sentence. What the coordinator maps each phrasing to:

- *"Keep refining a tagline for Acme until it's punchy and under 60 chars — stop when it's good or after 5 tries."* → `refine_start({ task: 'Write a tagline for Acme', eval: { kind: 'judge', rubric: 'punchy; under 60 characters' }, stop: { maxLoops: 5, targetScore: 0.9 } })`
- *"Loop this refactor 6 times with mistral, judged by deepseek."* → `refine_start({ task: 'Refactor: …', model: 'mistral', eval: { kind: 'judge', rubric: '…', judgeModel: 'deepseek' }, stop: { maxLoops: 6 } })`
- *"Try four different angles on the name and show me the best."* → `search_start({ task: 'Name …', seedCount: 4, eval: { … }, stop: { … } })`
- *"Keep working this until it meets our practices."* → `eval: { kind: 'practices', workDescription: '…' }`

Because runs are background jobs, the coordinator holds the handle and polls until done — you just see the result and its trace. (Distinct from the MCP **prompts** below: those are host-executed skill *templates*; the loops are *tools* you steer in natural language.)

## Prompts

The server also registers the guardrails **reasoning skills** (`corpus/skills/*.md`) as MCP prompts. Invoking a prompt returns the skill's instructions plus your arguments as a message for your own model to execute, so a skill runs on the host's tokens. The corpus source defaults to the canonical guardrails repo (override with `AIGENCY_GUARDRAILS_URL`); loading is best-effort, so a missing token simply means no skill prompts. Requires an MCP client that supports prompts.

## Caching semantics

`read_file` returns from cache on the second call for the same `(sourceUrl, ref, path)`. `grep`, `find_symbol`, and `code_graph` scan-and-cache on demand. `write_file` / `edit_file` invalidate the written file from the cache so the next read/search re-fetches.

Per-process state. New MCP process = empty cache. Workflow tools do not cache today.

## Errors

`SourceApiError` / `WorkflowApiError` map to MCP tool errors with status codes. 404 = missing resource. 401 = auth (check env vars + Trello Power-Up referer). 501 = operation not supported by the backend (e.g. Trello `parentId` patches). Worker tools never throw — a missing / unreachable worker returns a clear, actionable message.

## What this is NOT

- Not a sync engine.
- Not a code editor (reads + writes pass through adapters; no in-memory text-editing primitives).
- Not opinionated about backends — URL routing picks the implementation.

## License

Apache-2.0.
